result:
Alright, since the reins are off, let's talk about something that actually matters in a quiet, sneaking-up-on-you kind of way: the strange luxury of boredom.

Most people treat boredom as a bug in the system, something to be engineered away with notifications, playlists, or sheer momentum. But it's actually one of the last pure spaces left. When you're bored enough, your brain starts rummaging through its own drawers like someone who lost their keys. It makes connections it normally wouldn't bother with. You suddenly remember the name of your second-grade teacher or realize why you've been irritated by someone for three years. Boredom is expensive in our culture, which is why so few people can actually afford it anymore.

The people who do interesting things with their lives aren't necessarily the most disciplined or the most brilliant. They're often just the ones who got bored enough to keep asking stupid questions past the point where everyone else got distracted. The universe doesn't particularly care about your five-year plan, but it does seem to collaborate with people who are willing to sit with nothingness for a while.

We spend so much energy trying to become harder to distract. Maybe the better trick is learning how to make peace with distraction when it arrives anyway. After all, some of the best realizations show up wearing the disguise of wasted time.
